Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We are given a DECLARE model and asked to identify anomalies generate hypotheses and propose verification approaches

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Lets interpret the constraints 1 init Every trace must start with activity R This is an initialization constraint 2 existence There must be at least one occurrence of C in the trace This is an existence constraint for C 3 responded existence For activity E there must be an occurrence of A in the trace Note The constraint is defined for E and requires A to occur 4 precedence Activity C must be preceded by R This means that if C occurs then there must be an occurrence of R before it 5 noncoexistence Activities E and C cannot occur together in the same trace This is a negative constraint

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: However note that the intended process flow is R A E P N C

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The init constraint requires the trace to start with R which is consistent with the intended flow The existence constraint requires C to occur which is also intended The responded existence constraint requires that if E occurs then A must occur This is intended because we need to assign an adjuster before evaluation The precedence constraint requires that if C occurs then R must have occurred before it This is intended

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The noncoexistence constraint forbids E and C from coexisting But in the intended flow E occurs before C so they do coexist This constraint is contradictory to the intended flow because it disallows the sequence R A E C

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Additionally note that the model does not enforce the order of A and E The responded existence constraint only says that if E occurs then A must occur but not necessarily before E However the intended flow requires A before E So there is a potential anomaly the model does not enforce the order of A and E

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Also the model does not enforce the presence of A at all The responded existence constraint only requires A if E occurs But what if E does not occur Then A might not occur but then the process might skip evaluation However the existence constraint only requires C not E So the model allows traces without E and hence without A as long as they start with R and end with C This might be an anomaly because evaluation is an essential step in the intended flow

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Hypothesis 1 The noncoexistence constraint might have been intended to prevent the coexistence of E and C in a different context but it was mistakenly applied here Alternatively it might be a result of a misinterpretation of the business requirement For example maybe the business rule is that E and C cannot occur at the same time but the constraint is applied at the trace level

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Hypothesis 2 The lack of order constraint between A and E might be due to an oversight when specifying the model Alternatively the process might have been designed to allow flexibility in the order but the business requirement actually requires A before E

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Hypothesis 3 The absence of constraints for A and E might be due to the model being focused only on the beginning and end of the process R and C and the noncoexistence of E and C without considering the intermediate steps as mandatory

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: We can join claim events with claims on claim id to get the event log for each claim We can then look for traces claims that have certain sequences of activities

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Lets write queries to check for the anomalies 1 Check for claims that have both E and C which the model forbids but the intended process requires 2 Check for claims where E occurs before A which the model allows but the intended process does not 3 Check for claims that have C but no E and hence no A because A is required only if E occurs but we are concerned with the absence of E 4 Check for claims that have E but no A this should not happen according to the models responded existence constraint but lets check

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Query 1 Find claims that have both E and C to see if they coexist in the data SELECT claim_id FROM claim_events WHERE activity IN E C GROUP BY claim_id HAVING COUNT DISTINCT activity 2

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Query 2 Find claims where E occurs before A that is the timestamp of E is less than the timestamp of A for the same claim SELECT e1 claim_id FROM claim_events e1 JOIN claim_events e2 ON e1 claim_id e2 claim_id WHERE e1 activity E AND e2 activity A AND e1 timestamp less than e2 timestamp

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Query 3 Find claims that have C but no E SELECT c claim_id FROM claim_events c WHERE c activity C AND NOT EXISTS SELECT 1 FROM claim_events e WHERE e claim_id c claim_id AND e activity E

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Query 4 Find claims that have E but no A SELECT e claim_id FROM claim_events e WHERE e activity E AND NOT EXISTS SELECT 1 FROM claim_events a WHERE a claim_id e claim_id AND a activity A
